betterquesting:10.desc:8:
§6Particle Chamber: Collision Chamber§f

§aCollision Chambers smash two particle beams head-on to produce new particles!§f
The §bCollision Chamber§f is the largest QMD chamber multiblock. It accepts two particle beams from opposite faces, accelerates them inward, and collides them at the central target tile. The collision produces new particles, items, and fluids per the §bCollision Chamber Recipes§f.

§6Multiblock Structure (CollisionChamberLogic.java:62)§f:
- §bExterior§f: any size NxNxM (LengthX = LengthZ, square footprint), §cLengthX must be odd§f (3, 5, 7, …).
- §bCenter block§f: a §bParticle Chamber§f (target) tile at the geometric middle — this is the §bcollision point§f.
- §bBeam ports§f: typically §btwo or more§f §bParticle Chamber Beam Ports§f on opposite outer faces, each connected inward by a line of §bParticle Chamber Beam Blocks§f.
- §bAt least 1 INPUT beam port§f is required per the standard chamber logic; the second beam is typically also INPUT from the opposite face.
- §bAt least 1 Particle Chamber Energy Port§f (supplies RF for chamber operation).
- §bAt least 1 Particle Chamber Fluid Port§f for fluid in/out.
- §bInternal Coils & Detectors§f: detectors and coils may be placed inside the chamber interior for diagnostics or recipe gating.

§6Controller Recipe§f:
The §bCollision Chamber Controller§f (`qmd:collision_chamber_controller`) is crafted in a vanilla §bminecraft.crafting§f grid from:
- §bElite Plating§f (`nuclearcraft:part:3`)
- §bTough Alloy Ingot§f (`nuclearcraft:alloy:1`)
- §bElite Processor§f (`qmd:semiconductor:6`)
- §bParticle Chamber Casing§f (`qmd:particle_chamber_casing`)

§6Behavior in Operation (CollisionChamberLogic.java:660-690)§f:
- The chamber receives §btwo particle beams§f from its input ports and computes the §bfocus loss§f for each beam segment using `Equations.focusLoss(getBeamLength(), stack)`.
- At the §bcollision point§f, the two beams interact to produce new particle species, items, and fluids per the chamber recipe.
- Output particles exit through OUTPUT beam ports.

§6Common Recipe Categories§f:
- §bMatter-antimatter annihilation§f: proton + antiproton → pions, muons, photons
- §bElectron-positron annihilation§f: electron + positron → photon pairs
- §bHeavy ion collisions§f: high-energy heavy ion beams on heavy targets → exotic isotopes, superheavy elements

§cWarning§f: A Collision Chamber that is missing a target block, has no INPUT beam port, has no energy port, or is not square/odd-sized will fail the `isMachineWhole()` validation and refuse to form. The §cElite Processor§f requirement is steep — make sure your §bAdvanced Processor§f and §bP-Type Doped Silicon§f production lines are stable first.

§eTip§f: A §b5×5×5§f Collision Chamber is the smallest legal size and ideal for first collisions. Use a §bBeam Splitter§f to derive the second beam from the same LINAC source — the splitter's straight-through and perpendicular outputs naturally feed the two opposite faces of the chamber.
